data.title: Teledyne: Re-Rated As Belief In Long-Term Compounding Returns
data.description:
Shares of Teledyne Technologies (TDY) have risen sharply to fresh highs, as improved fundamentals of the business are filtering through in the business performance and its outlook.

In fact, some of this momentum was seen arriving late in 2024 already, when I last looked at shares of Teledyne, this was a $480 stock at the time.

Teledyne is a great long-term value creator but stepped away from its typical bolt-on M&A strategy to create shareholder value with a much larger deal for FLIR. This was followed by some underwhelming recent operating performance, making some investors question if the long-term value creation model was broken. With solid operating momentum returning, shares have rightfully commanded a premium again, leaving no immediate opportunities.

About Teledyne - Everywhere You Look

Teledyne develops technology that enables sensing, analyzing, and distribution of information, the business describing itself as a global sensing and decision-support company. The company claims to have a broad and diversified business, consisting of highly engineered products. The company is a real shareholder value creator, driven by these operating capabilities being combined with solid capital allocation decisions.

The company generates some $6.1 billion in annual sales, with results reported across four segments. The largest of these is digital imaging, a segment representing half of sales, as segment margins are seen in the mid- to high double-digits. Products cover all wavelengths of the spectrum with this segment carefully crafted by numerous acquisitions over the past decades.

Instrumentation constitutes a quarter of sales, these being very lucrative sales with margins reported in the high twenties. Of interest is a rapidly growing and just over $1 billion aerospace & defense business, although that growth has been aided by M&A here as well. These are very lucrative activities with margins reported in the twenties. All this is complemented by smaller, lumpier, and lower-margin +$400 million engineered systems.

About half of sales are generated in the US, split pretty evenly between commercial and government markets, complemented by European and Asian activities, predominantly.

The company has increased sales tenfold over the past quarter, aided by more than 70 separate M&A deals, combined reported at a +$12 billion cost. Many of these were smaller deals, but the outlier was a much larger deal for FLIR at an $8 billion price tag, as average M&A deals typically run in the tens of millions, often hundreds of millions at best.

Besides growing sales, EBITDA margins have improved from low single digits to levels in the mid-twenties, only adding to the operating performance demonstrated upon, with earnings having risen from less than a dollar to about $20 per share.

Back To The Fall Of 2024

Following the integration of FLIR, the company was dealing with a tougher environment, this being the environment in which inflation was high in a post-pandemic world. This made it so that 2023 sales rose by just 3%, as growth was seen up to 4% in 2024, although aided by its bolt-on M&A playbook. While this growth looks solid, it felt soft given the past performance of Teledyne and its track record.

However, shares have risen to a high around $480 per share by the fall, after revenues were actually down in the first half of 2024, causing pressure on both the sales and earnings guidance. However, topline sales growth returned to positive 3% in the third quarter, following some orders being booked, as earnings were seen around $19.35 per share for 2024. A net debt load of $2.2 billion worked down to a 2 times leverage ratio, valuing equity at $23 billion and the enterprise at $25 billion.

Besides some bolt-on dealmaking, a larger $710 million deal to acquire the aerospace & defense electronics business from Excelitas was announced in the fall of 2024. With earnings seen near $20 per share while shares traded near the $500 mark, earnings multiples had risen to 24-25 times earnings, while leverage was seen ticking up quite a bit towards 3 times, as the organic performance felt a bit soft.

Rocketing Higher

Shares of Teledyne ended up falling victim to the trade tariff talks in the spring of last year, falling to the lower $400s. However, shares rose to the near $600 mark in the fall of that year, corrected to the $500 mark by year-end, and now have rallied to $620 again as investors embraced the fourth quarter results being released.

After ending 2024 on a stronger note, full-year adjusted earnings ended up increasing four cents to $19.73 per share, aided by M&A efforts and growth. The company guided for 2025 earnings to advance to $21.10-$21.50 per share.

After posting an impressive more than 7% increase in first quarter sales, the company announced another bolt-on deal, as the company acquired Maretron assets from Littlefuse (LFUS), with few details announced. Second quarter sales ended up increasing more than 10%, as later that summer the company announced the acquisition of the TransponderTech business from Saab, with no details announced at the time. After third-quarter sales rose by almost 7%, Teledyne started 2026 with another bolt-on deal, this time acquiring UK-based DD-Scientific.

Hitting New highs

Teledyne just announced strong results for the end of 2025, causing shares to jump 10% to $620 per share. Fourth quarter sales rose by more than 7%, with full-year sales up nearly 8% to $6.12 billion. Acquisitions contributed about $270 million of this growth, or just over 4%, with reported growth split pretty evenly between organic and inorganic achievements.

The company leveraged all this pretty well, with adjusted earnings up some 11% to $21.99 per share, as M&A is seen continuing to contribute to 2026 growth. This came after M&A picked up in 2025, with spending on acquisitions reported at $821 million. Net debt is reported at $2.1 billion, as I peg EBITDA just over $1.4 billion, for a leverage ratio around 1.5 times, as Teledyne itself even reports a 1.4 times leverage ratio.

The company guided for continued growth this year, with earnings seen advancing to $23.65 per share, plus or minus twenty cents. Earnings growth is seen outpacing sales growth, with sales seen moving up just about 4% to $6.37 billion, which feels a bit soft amidst incremental M&A activity, certainly amidst a 1.08 times book-to-bill ratio reported in 2025.

Given this momentum and a solid outlook, the business has found itself on a stronger footing, with the company returning to mid-single-digit organic growth rates. This is accompanied by bolt-on M&A, which the company beautifully calls ¨string of pearl¨ acquisition strategy, driving growth.

This, however, has pushed up earnings multiples to 28 times earnings based on 2025 earnings and 26 times forward earnings, as the adjusted earnings numbers look quite clean, in all fairness.

Given the great track record and improved momentum, I understand why some multiple expansion has been seen. With a net earnings yield just near 4%, investors are betting heavily on growth (with no dividends paid), making me a bit reserved. This also follows shares having risen just 25% in the time frame of a quarter. However, with renewed momentum seen in the business, I am eager to revisit the thesis on dips here.

data.title: Palantir's Revenue Surge: Don't Let P/E Ratios Fool You
data.description:
When I last covered Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) on October 14th, 2025 (with a “buy” rating) in my article “Palantir: $1 Billion Quarter Changes Everything”, the stock was beginning to lose some of the bullish momentum that had characterized share price trends during the prior six-month period. Given the massive, longer-term rallies that this stock has managed to generate, it wasn’t completely surprising to see that some analysts were suggesting that it might be a good time to start taking profits. However, in my article, I argued that I was maintaining my positive outlook because the customer count and revenue growth rate in Palantir’s U.S. commercial segment would probably continue driving the stock higher into the end of 2025.

Unfortunately, this did not turn out to be the case because PLTR shares are actually slightly lower now (despite the small gains of 5.1% that have been provided by the S&P 500 during the same period of time). At this stage, I think investors can look at this activity in one of two ways. First, and more negatively, it can be argued that the stock’s upward momentum is gone and that the “gravity” of overextended valuations is ready to take over. Second, more enthusiastic investors might argue that the stock is simply basing for the next run higher. I will explain why I believe the positive side of the argument appears to be stronger, and I will outline potential price levels that will indicate a reversal point in the long-term uptrend if my bullish thesis turns out to be incorrect.

Palantir’s prior earnings results were quite strong, with beats on both the top and bottom lines. The total revenue figure indicated YoY growth of 63% and surpassed $1 billion once again. Net income results were even more impressive (at $475.6 million), which implied astronomical YoY gains of more than 231%. Revenue from the commercial business improved by more than 2x (at $397 million), and contract values for closed deals in the segment saw an expansion greater than 4x (at $1.31 billion). These figures were released not long after new partnerships with Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Snowflake Inc. (SNOW) were announced, so I would say that it’s somewhat surprising that all of this activity failed to ignite a surge in share prices.

The reality is that the stock has essentially flatlined since then, even though full-year sales guidance has been raised to $4.4 billion with $1.9 - 2.1 billion in free cash flow now expected for the period. In some sense, this should force PLTR investors to take a step back and question what exactly is going on here, because when a stock fails to produce substantive gains when a wide swath of positive information is released, what is going to occur if the next earnings report shows evidence of weakness? Palantir’s valuation issues are the most likely explanation for the stock’s inability to move higher into the end of 2025. But I disagree (at least to some extent) with the metrics that are typically cited when having these types of discussions.

I’ll start with a metric that doesn’t seem to get very much attention, which is Palantir’s forward PEG ratio. Rather than simply looking at the stock’s nosebleed-level P/E ratio, PLTR does not look anywhere near as overvalued when we add a growth component to the equation. Not only is Palantir’s PEG ratio below the 1.0 threshold (which, on its own, suggests that expectations for earnings gains are enough to justify an elevated P/E), but the stock is also trading near the mid-point of the peer group. On one hand, we can see that CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and Okta, Inc. (OKTA) might be better positioned at the moment. But Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT) is much more expensive, and Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) is trading in roughly similar territory. On balance, Palantir is still considered to be roughly in line with industry averages, and this shows that a simple look at P/E alone is not enough to fairly analyze PLTR.

Palantir’s trend in absolute free cash flow is also improving, with current numbers that are now printing toward the top of the peer group. Indeed, Fortinet is still looking stronger in this regard. But I am much more concerned with the underlying trend than I am with the absolute figures. As long as Palantir can continue showing expansion in free cash flow, it will remain clear that the company is scaling into a major cash generator. This is important because expansion of cash flow is a factor that can compress valuation multiples—even if share prices do not see substantial declines. If we are going to expect PLTR to maintain its long-term uptrend, I think this might be one of the most critical metrics to watch because it can still justify the bullish momentum.

Now, on the negative side, I do need to mention that Palantir’s free cash flow yield is an obvious risk because it is the lowest in this industry grouping. What this tells us is that the market has already priced in most of the company’s expected cash flow figure. To be sure, this is quite an unforgiving metric, and it implies that even the slightest miss in quarterly performance can cause a dramatic re-rating in the share price.

To settle this debate, I would argue that investors should finally consider the differences between Palantir’s trailing and forward price-sales ratios. Here, there is a wide gap between the forward and TTM figures (roughly 65x vs. 112x). Interestingly, this forward compression essentially proves that the market expects to see significant improvements in the underlying sales trends. If share prices continue trading with this flat trajectory (as we saw into the end of 2025), Palantir’s “elevated” multiples can compress by themselves. Moreover, forward multiples that are significantly lower will typically suggest that a business is still working through a hyper-growth phase that the market expects to continue.

Of course, none of this is a guarantee, and with a stock like Palantir (that is “priced to perfection”), there is always the potential for quarterly reports to offer an injection of extreme volatility. The next earnings release is scheduled for February 2nd, with consensus estimates for GAAP EPS calling for $0.18 on $1.34 billion in revenue. Normally, I would not place as much emphasis on analyst expectations for the top-line and bottom-line figures. But when we are dealing with a stock that has a heavy (and very loyal) retail following and share price gains of more than 2,200% in less than three years, it should be understood that wild and volatile price swings can occur if quarterly results fail to match expectations.

Personally, I will be watching for changes in free cash flow expectations because this is the metric that I am using as a basis for large portions of my total outlook. In terms of real share price action, critical support zones have developed near $148.20 (which marks the lows from November 21st, 2025, and the Neckline of a potential Head and Shoulders pattern). There is also a confluence here with the 200-Day exponential moving average, so I think that any downside break of this area could signal a longer-term reversal in the bullish trend. Of course, that area has not yet broken, and a Head and Shoulders pattern is not actually “triggered” unless there is a downside breach of the neckline.

Conversely, positive surprises in the upcoming earnings report should help to keep this support zone intact. If this does turn out to be true, the next major area of resistance comes into play near $207.50, which marks the highs from November 3rd, 2025. As a result, I do think that PLTR stock is probably trading near an inflection point. Sideways trading activity tends to characterize periods of indecision, so there is a lot riding on the next quarterly release. From a trading perspective, I would let the charts do most of the work because these aforementioned price levels are likely to work as major indicators of whether the long-term uptrend actually has the potential to continue through the first half of 2026.

data.title: Kura Oncology: Safety Profile Of Komzifti Supports My Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
On December 4, 2025, I initiated coverage of Kura Oncology (KURA) in this article with a Speculative Buy rating.

The core of that thesis was straightforward. Kura is an early commercial stage biotech. In November 2025, the FDA approved its lead drug, Komzifti (ziftomenib), for relapsed or refractory NPM1-mutated acute myeloid leukemia [AML]. Under the current label, the realistic U.S. market opportunity is on the order of $350–$400 million annually.

The long-term value of Kura’s equity is not really about relapsed AML. It is about whether Komzifti can work in frontline AML, where the patient population is far larger and where patients often stay on therapy much longer. Frontline AML represents a three to six billion-dollar annual potential market in the United States alone. That is where enduring equity value would ultimately be created.

The reason Komzifti has a credible shot at that opportunity lies in its safety profile and biological positioning. Komzifti is a menin inhibitor. While not first in class, it has demonstrated a comparatively clean cardiac profile, particularly with respect to QT prolongation.

That detail is critical as modern AML treatment is built around drug combinations, and a menin inhibitor will not become part of frontline care unless it can be safely layered with venetoclax-based regimens, intensive chemotherapy, and FLT3 inhibitors. Komzifti’s profile materially improves its probability of being used in such combinations.

In my original article, I noted that the value of Kura as a company would ultimately be determined by the outcome of its frontline expansion strategy; therefore, an investment in Kura’s common stock is highly risky, although some risks are mitigated by an FDA-approved molecule and $501 million in cash held by the company as of Sept 30, 2025.

I noted the company has a profit-sharing arrangement with Kyowa Kirin, which any investor of Kura must take into account when projecting its future profits and stock valuation.

I also alluded in that article to the fact that CEO Troy Wilson purchased 100,000 shares at $7.13 per share in the open market in August and September 2025 at prices not far from where the stock trades today.

Those purchases were real cash purchases. In light of the subsequent volatility, the stock has effectively round-tripped back toward the levels at which Wilson himself was buying.

Stock Volatility and a Puzzling Price Decline

Since that article, Kura’s stock has declined from roughly $12 to around $8 today, dealing with its investors a short-term setback.

The selloff may have been driven by the company’s disclosure through this SEC Form 8-K filing of $2.1 million in net product revenue during the initial five-week commercial rollout of Komzifti, ended December 31, 2025. But that disclosure was made on January 11, 2026 — well after the stock had already fallen. The timing simply does not line up.

In fact, shortly before Christmas, Kura’s stock spiked more than 10% after Jim Cramer made the following comments on national television about Komzifti and Kura:

“I have always felt that if you have something that is for cancer and it works, then you have to own the stock as a speculation, but just as a speculation, because it’s been such a difficult disease.”

The subsequent slide back toward $8, therefore, remains mysterious to me. I welcome any comments below as to the causes of such price decline.

In any case, in small- and mid-cap biotech, prices often move hard without known triggers, driven as much by flows and mood as by any specific piece of news. Volatility is the core terrain that we may exploit.

What the December 8, 2025 ASH Presentation Actually Showed

Another potential cause of the stock price decline may be the company’s presentation at ASH on December 8, 2025, the slides of which can be found here.

During that event, the company presented Phase 1b data from KOMET-007 showing that ziftomenib combined with venetoclax and azacitidine produced:

• High remission rates in newly diagnosed NPM1-mutated AML (86% composite complete remission; 73% complete remission); • High rates of molecular MRD negativity; • Durable responses with a median duration of remission and overall survival not yet reached at the time of cutoff; and • A safety profile comparable to venetoclax/azacitidine alone, with only one interim QT-related event reported, which resolved, allowing the treatment to continue.

These data were used to justify advancement into the ongoing KOMET-017 randomized Phase 3 frontline study evaluating ziftomenib-based regimens in both intensive and non-intensive settings.

I do not see anything in this presentation that would naturally explain a material decline in the stock thereafter. If anything, the presentation confirmed the scientific case underlying Kura’s frontline clinical and consequently commercial potential.

The Reasons for My Upgrade

While the scientific foundation of the story has not been eroded, the valuation setup is different now. At today’s price, Kura’s market capitalization is approximately $750 million.

According to the same Form 8-K, the company held approximately $667 million in cash and equivalents as of December 31, 2025, including $195 million of milestone payments received in the fourth quarter of 2025 under its collaboration with Kyowa Kirin.

The company reports in its latest Form 10-Q approximately $468 million of liabilities, $317 million of which represented the contract liabilities under its partnership with Kyowa. These amounts reflect portions of the transaction price already received by the company that are allocated to future development and commercialization services the company is obligated to perform. They are not borrowings that must be repaid with cash.

Taken together, the market is currently valuing Kura’s operating business—including its FDA-approved product, commercial platform, and pipeline—at roughly $80 million (after accounting for cash), subject to $317 million in collaboration-related performance obligations plus $151 million in conventional debt obligations.

Third, the same Form 8-K reported $2.1 million of Komzifti net product revenue in the initial five-week commercial period. Even if one simply annualizes that early run rate—assuming no growth at all—Komzifti would generate approximately $21 million of annual net product revenue. That assumption is extremely conservative, as oncology launches are typically gradual and back-end loaded. That level of revenue shall support the market’s current valuation of the assets of the company subject to its indebtedness given the special nature of the company's debts, without assigning substantial value to the launch ramp of Komzifti or to the much larger frontline opportunity.

Fourth, the company also expects to recognize approximately $15–$17 million of collaboration revenue in Q4 2025. While non-cash, this further underscores the fact that collaboration structure may appear to create debt obligations upon the company; it also generates GAAP revenues and profits for the company.

What has changed is the stock price. At today’s level, investors are effectively paying substantially less for the assets, including the cash of the company and the frontline opportunity. Viewed in that light, the setup is materially more favorable than it was when I first wrote about Kura.

In short, the scientific and valuation foundations of my original thesis remain intact: Komzifti is an FDA-approved menin inhibitor with a safety profile that plausibly supports frontline combination use, and Kura has the financial resources and institutional knowledge to pursue that opportunity. For these reasons, I am upgrading Kura Oncology from Buy to Strong Buy.

data.title: This Isn't A Bubble At All - It's A Stock Picker's Paradise
data.description:
Introduction

I don’t think I’m breaking any news when I make the case that the market feels a bit uncomfortable right now. One reason for that is the market’s elevated valuation. That’s a topic we spent a lot of time discussing last year as well.

Usually, valuations are one of the biggest triggers for people to get either excited or fearful. After all, I think I can speak for everyone reading this when I say that we all try to buy assets below what we consider to be the fair value. If that weren’t the case, we wouldn’t buy them.

Moreover, many of the older investors among us have been through a number of cycles. I was just a kid in 2000, but I can imagine how buying at an elevated valuation when “everyone” said that the market would be fine has resulted in losses and years without real gains.

Using the data below, we can say that every dollar invested in the S&P 500 at the 2000 Dot-com peak needed roughly 14 years to break even on an inflation-adjusted basis.

So yeah, I fully get it when people are quick to become defensive whenever they see bubble indicators flash red. However, that’s also when big mistakes are made.

The tricky part is that, in hindsight, bubbles always look obvious, and everyone can monitor valuation indicators. That may drive sentiment, but I don’t think it does this market any justice.

That’s what this article is all about, as I will give you a market update, explain why this isn’t a bubble, and why I remain so excited to put capital to work.

So, let’s get to it!

Yes, Valuations Are an Issue

I may have a rather unusual thesis every now and then, but I’m not naive.

Valuations aren’t favorable.

While on a short-term basis, it’s fair to say that valuations don’t matter, data also support that on a long-term basis (10-year forward returns), valuations tend to matter a lot. Right now, the data tells us the market may show zero annual returns for at least a full decade.

That’s in theory. Also, note that this is based on just one data set, which is the market’s P/E ratio. However, this is getting support from other valuation metrics, including the Shiller CAPE. As Apollo reports (using the chart below):

The cyclically adjusted price earnings ratio, also known as the Shiller P/E, is a stock market valuation measure that divides the current stock price by the average of the last 10 years’ inflation-adjusted earnings, thereby smoothing out business cycle volatility to assess long-term over/undervaluation and predict future returns. It provides a better gauge of sustainable earnings power than the traditional P/E ratio, which uses only one year’s earnings. The latest reading shows that equity valuations are near the highest levels since 1880 [...]. - Apollo Global Management

In other words, so far, we have a flashing warning signal from the P/E ratio and one of the highest Shiller CAPE ratios since 1880. I can imagine, by now, you’re curious what I could possibly come up with to turn this into a bullish article.

It’s All About the Bigger Picture

One of the things we have discussed a number of times is that this market isn’t a “normal” market. Going into this year, the ten biggest stocks accounted for more than 40.0% of the entire S&P 500. That’s one of the highest numbers ever. As these companies account for 32.5% of the market’s earnings, we know that they have been a core driver of the market’s unfavorable valuation.

NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for roughly 7% of the market.

Because any Mag-7 stocks were re-rated higher due to AI, the entire market benefited. To give you some proof, or at least some indication that supports my statement, I produced the chart below, which shows the ratio between the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) and the market-weighted S&P 500. The moment AI went mainstream, it caused the market to become more concentrated, causing the market-weighted S&P 500 (the “normal” S&P 500) to outperform.

That means at least two things:

  • This is likely a market of stocks instead of a stock market, as many companies may not have high valuations.
  • The market’s optimism that caused the re-rating of growth stocks doesn’t have to become bearish.

I believe the second bullet point is key, as AI isn’t a bearish factor. As I have written before, I believe we are now at a point where AI benefits are spreading from hyperscalers to the broader economy. Earnings may confirm that. Using the chart below, it’s time for two more bullet points:

  • Overall earnings growth is expected to remain strong. Combining both the Mag-7 and the rest of the S&P 500, we’re likely looking at prolonged growth, with each quarter of 2026 seeing double-digit EPS growth. When adding that AI could continue to enhance long-term margins, it supports the market’s valuation until the growth story starts to show cracks.
  • The S&P 493 (ignoring the Mag-7) is expected to see a growth acceleration. By the end of 2026, the rest of the market could show almost equal earnings growth to the S&P 500.

So far, this is bullish, with the biggest risk being that any growth headwinds could have an amplified impact on the market’s correction. I’m making that case because elevated valuations aren’t an issue as long as earnings growth is fine. In other words, while I expect the market to be fine, I also expect more volatility, as any negative headlines could create doubt and fears of overvaluation.

This is Basically About Two Markets

Essentially, we’re talking about two markets. One is the growth-focused market. The other market is the “real economy” market. Only one of these is associated with bubble talk right now.

One of the most important factors going forward is the business cycle. If we are in a late-style cycle, odds are even the “real economy” companies are on thin ice. After all, when economic growth goes south, there’s no point in trying to diversify beyond Big Tech. In such a scenario, the Mag-7 becomes attractive again, as these companies have terrific balance sheets and less cyclical risk.

The good news is that this isn’t a late-cycle economy.

As some readers may know, I have often talked about a cyclical recovery. In fact, while I expect “higher for longer” inflation, I see no stagflation. I have bet on the exact opposite happening, meaning both higher growth and higher inflation.

For now, that thesis is looking good and getting new confirmation.

Apollo Global Management even calls it an “Industrial Renaissance,” supported by a substantial boom in non-defense capital goods. Growth in that area has hit a new multi-year high after bottoming in 2023 and 2024. Both of these years saw a transportation recession, housing weakness, and other headwinds for a wide range of cyclical companies.

More evidence of this is the ongoing weakness in the leading ISM Manufacturing Index. As we can see below, if I’m right about this being a volatile bottoming process, we’re in the early stages of a cyclical expansion, not in the late cycle of a past boom. The evidence simply doesn’t support that.

In fact, I would even make the case that early-cycle environments tend to look exactly like this. In such environments, fundamentals start to turn, policy remains supportive, and investors hesitate because backward-looking indicators still look messy (sentiment).

Policy, for example, includes the president’s efforts to kickstart housing growth by proposing $200 billion in mortgage purchases to suppress interest rates. That’s both inflationary (it boosts liquidity and demand) and another piece of evidence that this administration is very serious about kickstarting economic growth.

One could even make the case that weakness in key areas like housing and manufacturing is bullish, as it triggers a response from policymakers and/or even the Fed. This also means many of these stocks are not overvalued yet, allowing us to buy quality at a value price.

That’s why I’m so excited.

We’re starting to see a confirmation in key indicators.

As we can see below, year-to-date, materials, industrials, and energy are all beating the S&P 500 by a wide margin. I expect this to continue, which is why I invested the majority of my new capital last year in the industrial and energy space.

Although I’m not throwing fresh capital at the rally after deploying more capital, I am rebuilding my cash position (by accumulating more new cash instead of selling other assets) to buy any correction that can help me add to cyclical stocks like Carlisle Companies (CSL), which is one of my favorite housing suppliers; Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), which is my favorite trucking company; and players like TransDigm (TDG) that benefit from both cyclical and secular growth in aerospace. These are just a few ideas, but I think you now know where my head is at by now and what that could mean for you.

Hence, instead of trying to time the market in general, I have deployed more capital than ever over the past 14 months. All of these investments were made in companies with attractive valuations while the market traded at a lofty valuation. This excludes my investments made during the April/May sell-off last year due to the trade war.

Or, to put it differently, I am not chasing momentum or dismissing valuation risks but simply recognizing that this market isn’t homogeneous but a mix of super unique companies at the start of a potential cyclical recovery.

Call me crazy (I’m sure some of you will do that in the comments), but I like my odds despite the obvious headwinds.

Takeaway

This market looks very expensive. The good news is that this is mainly the case at the index level. This isn’t a homogeneous bubble, if you ask me. Valuations are massively distorted due to the market’s imbalance, which has created a lot of hidden opportunities in an economy that could see a cyclical upswing.

In tricky environments like this, being cautious is important, but it could also be a costly mistake. So, instead of avoiding equities, I think we should spend more time finding overlooked opportunities. Needless to say, I’ll help you with that and provide a lot more actionable ideas going forward.

Risks to My Thesis

The margin of error is slim at this point. My thesis relies on a cyclical recovery and strong S&P 500 earnings growth. Without it, it’s nearly impossible to defend the market’s valuation. That’s why even increasing expectations of a recession could trigger a steep correction and why I expect volatility to see a few spikes this year, as these valuations could amplify bad news.

data.title: Exelixis: High-Growth Oncology Name Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe that there is unfairly little attention focused on Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL) at Seeking Alpha, whereas in my opinion this stock has one of the highest growth potentials in the medium term. Exelixis can be described as a fast-growing business in the field of pharmacology, which manages to simultaneously maintain high-growth rates without sacrificing margins. In addition, the company supports its growth purely with its own money, without attracting debt funds and maintaining the debt burden at almost zero. The company's key products (Cabometyx) are confidently leading in their markets, and in addition to them, management is developing several more promising new drugs. The most interesting thing is that any company with such parameters would have long been trading at huge multiplier premiums to its peers. But not in the case of Exelixis—the stock price is currently adequate, and in some places there is even an underestimation. For all these characteristics, I like this company. I assign a "Strong Buy" rating to the company's shares with a target price of $55 and a growth potential of 25% in the baseline scenario over the year horizon.

Still A One-Product Story - But That’s Changing

I always try to tell you in detail about the company when I write about it for the first time. This is done primarily for readers unfamiliar with the company. In this chapter, I will try to briefly, in simple words, but in detail, describe how Exelixis operates.

As a pharmaceutical company, Exelixis is engaged in the development, manufacture, and sale of drugs for the treatment of cancer. The company has its own flagship product, and another promising drug is being prepared for market release. Almost all of the revenue is generated by sales of Cabometyx, a drug that helps cure certain types of cancer, in particular kidney cancer and rarer hormone-related tumors. This drug firmly occupies a leading position in its niche. It is noteworthy that last year it began to be used for the treatment of some other types of cancer, which allowed the drug to expand its presence in related niches. Outside the United States, the company provides a license to sell the drug to other companies, for which it receives its royalty income. The management, aware of all the risks of dependence on one drug, invests a lot of money in R&D in order to diversify its revenue sources. The main contender for this role and the most promising drug is zanzalintinib. It treats the most common types of bowel cancer, has recently successfully passed important trials, and is preparing to enter the U.S. market soon. There are four more promising drugs that, if the circumstances are right, can be marketed, but they are only at the development stage.

In order to accelerate the development of new drugs and increase the cost effectiveness of R&D, the management decided in August to appoint Dr. Dana T. Aftab as the new head. He has been working at the company since 1998 and has extensive experience in the development of pharmaceutical products. In August, the Board of Directors decided to slightly reorganize the business in order to better control costs. As a result of the decision, more than 130 people were laid off and the office in Pennsylvania was closed. Investors generally reacted positively to such changes. I believe that management is moving in the right direction. The strong results did not turn the management's head. On the wave of success, they could uncontrollably increase SG&A expenses, arguing for high business growth rates. As you can see, the opposite is true—management strictly controls costs in order to keep business margins high. In my opinion, this is commendable.

Q3 Outperformance Validates Business Momentum

Data for the fourth quarter and the whole of 2025 will be released in mid-February. I suggest analyzing the results for the third quarter in order to understand the current state of the company and formulate adequate expectations for the future report.

Revenue for Q3 increased by almost 11% YoY. The result is excellent and indicates a high demand for Cabometyx. In a press release, management emphasized that there was no price effect on revenue growth—the price of the drug remained virtually unchanged during the quarter.

The Cabometyx product itself continues to steadily gain market share in kidney cancer drugs. It is likely that as early as 2026, the market share will be above 50%, as the entire market is growing much slower than the sales growth of Cabometyx. This is due to the fact that other tumors are successfully treated with this drug, and it also combines well with other medications.

As a result, the growth rate of the Exelixis business significantly exceeds the growth rate of competitors from the sector. In fact, Exelixis is one of the best companies in terms of growth and business margins at the same time. This is rarely seen even in the most dynamically developing industries.

As can be seen from the table, the company demonstrates a very high level of profitability. At the same time, Exelixis directs most competitors to CapEx, which allows it to keep its free cash flow high.

The margin improvement trend continued in Q3. For the third quarter in a row, management has been diligently reducing SG&A costs. The same goes for R&D. Here, expenses decreased by 11% yoy against the background of the completion of expensive research phases. At the same time, it is important for a company to diversify its sources of income, so it will most likely have to increase its R&D expenses one way or another. Otherwise, you may lose out in competition if your closest competitors spend more money on development and eventually develop more effective drugs.

Net income increased by more than 60% in both GAAP and non-GAAP terms. EPS increased to $0.69 GAAP and $0.78 Non-GAAP, while in Q3’24 it was only $0.40 and $0.47, respectively. EPS growth was simultaneously impacted by revenue growth, cost cuts, and aggressive buybacks. The market had expected EPS of about $0.68–$0.69 and was pleasantly surprised by the strong outperformance of consensus forecasts. The consensus revenue forecast was also lower than the final result. Looking at so many strong results, management updated its forecasts for Q4 to improve its key financial indicators.

Undervalued Leader With Structural Advantages

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030, discounting the forecast FCFs, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and terminal value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

In my model, I assume that the revenue CAGR for 2025-2030 will be about 8-9%, compared to 21-22% for 2020-2024. This is due to the gradual decline in demand for the main product, the emergence of alternative drugs, and the consolidation of the market. After 2027, the share of Cabometyx will gradually decrease due to the introduction of new drugs. They will be the main drivers of growth. Most likely, Exelixis will begin actively acquiring new companies and raising debt in the next two to three years to support business growth and diversify its product line. Operating margins will continue to grow due to tight cost control; however, with the gradual decline in demand for Cabometyx, the need for R&D expenses will grow stronger and stronger. Therefore, the operating margin will grow slowly and will grow to 37-38% in the baseline scenario. The company spends little money on CapEx, compared to its peers. In my opinion, this trend will continue, and management will try to keep CapEx's share of revenue at 2-3%. As a result, the average FCF yield in 2025-2030 will be 27%, compared to 13% in the period 2020-2024.

I got the WACC for Exelixis at 7.04% (all prerequisites are listed in the table above).

The most surprising thing for me is the evaluation of the company. Exelixis is one of the best companies both in terms of business growth and profitability. It has the lowest debt burden among its competitors and conducts aggressive buybacks. With such parameters in the American stock market, stocks usually trade at huge premiums to the sector and their historical averages. But not in the case of Exelixis. For many multiples, we see large discounts of 20-30% compared to the sectoral averages. In my opinion, this is a clear underestimation of the company's shares.

The results of my DCF model fully confirm my thesis about undervaluation in stocks. In the base scenario, the share price should be in the range of $52-52, which is an upside of 18-25%. In more positive scenarios (the WACC is lower and the multipliers are higher), the growth potential is 32-38%. Interestingly, even in the most negative scenario, the stock price could rise by 6-13%. I recommend buying Exelixis shares with a target price of $55 per share and an upside of 25%.

Aggressive Buybacks Signal Confidence

There is practically no debt burden on the business. The total debt at the end of September was $177 million, with about $988 million in cash in the accounts, which results in negative net debt. Interest expenses are near zero and do not put pressure on net profit.

The fact that the company manages to maintain high business growth rates without attracting debt is a great advantage. Exelixis does not pay dividends, but aggressively buys shares from the market. In total, from 2023 to Q3 2025, the company repurchased almost $1.9 billion worth of its own shares at an average price of $26.84, greatly reducing the number of shares outstanding. In October 2025, management announced a new share repurchase program for up to $750 million by the end of 2026. At the current share price, this is equivalent to buying back 15-17 million shares or 5% of the total capitalization. Management is not going to review its strategy of paying profits to shareholders. It doesn't make much sense—everything is working well. But at some point, the company will have to raise debt in order to support growth. Most likely, Exelixis will want to enter the M&A market and make several strategic purchases to diversify its revenues. Then debt financing will be attracted. Dividends will begin to be paid in the distant future, when Exelixis will become a mature company.

Risks

The main risk is related to the complete dependence of the business on Cabometyx. The emergence of generics and alternative drugs from competitors can severely affect revenues from Cabometyx and, accordingly, the entire revenue of the business. At the same time, the success of new drugs is not guaranteed—this is the next risk. Zanzalintinib is a promising drug, but commercial success may be worse than expected. All other risks are related in one way or another to possible failures in development and regulatory changes in the United States, which may negatively affect the price of drugs.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, this is one of the best investment ideas in the pharmaceutical market. The company's flagship drug is aggressively capturing market share, while Exelixis is preparing to bring a promising new drug to the market. The company itself is noticeably ahead of its competitors in the sector in terms of revenue growth and business profitability, managing to keep its debt burden at almost zero and aggressively buying shares from the market. I believe that those who are interested in shares of pharmaceutical companies should pay attention to Exelixis. My rating is Strong Buy.
